title: Worker that enforces at-most-once semantics via database unique index
prompt: Write a pair of files: (1) an Ecto migration `priv/repo/migrations/20260401_create_job_executions.exs` that creates a `job_executions` table with columns `job_identifier (string, unique)`, `completed_at (utc_datetime_usec)`, `timestamps()`. (2) A worker `MyApp.Workers.IdempotentEventWorker` in `lib/my_app/workers/idempotent_event_worker.ex` that takes `%{"event_id" => id}`. On each execution, it wraps the side effect in an `Ecto.Multi` that (a) inserts a row into `job_executions` with `job_identifier: "event:#{id}"` using `Multi.insert/3` on an unmarked changeset — on unique violation, return `{:error, :already_done}`, (b) runs the side effect via `MyApp.Events.process(id)`, (c) updates `completed_at`. If `{:error, :already_done}` bubbles up, perform/1 returns `:ok`. Otherwise return `:ok` or `{:error, reason}`. Write both files.
tier_rationale: Database-level idempotency with a dedicated ledger table + Multi handling of the unique-violation race. Production-grade pattern.
research_citation: DZone: 'Idempotency in AI Tools: The Most Expensive Mistake' — 'Retrying a request three times without idempotency guarantees potentially triples what should have been one operation.'
